title: Chinese space program
body:
China has one of the most active space programs in the world. With launch vehicles of the Long March rocket family and four spaceports (Jiuquan, Taiyuan, Xichang, Wenchang), China conducts the most or second most orbital launches each year. China's fleet of over 1,300 Earth orbit satellites serves communication, navigation, reconnaissance and scientific research. The China Manned Space Program operates Tiangong, one of two active space stations alongside the International Space Station (ISS). China has achieved robotic rover, lander, and orbiter missions to the Moon and Mars. 
From the 1950s, aided by the allied Soviet Union, development began on China's nuclear weapons, Dongfeng ballistic missiles, and Long March rockets, honored as the Two Bombs, One Satellite projects. Despite setbacks from the Cultural Revolution and Sino-Soviet split, China became the fifth country to independently launch a satellite, Dong Fang Hong 1, in 1970. China is one of three countries with a human spaceflight capability since Shenzhou 5 in 2003. China commercially launched Western satellites from 1988, but failures, especially Intelsat 708, caused the US to embargo satellite export in 1998. After the US vetoed its joining the ISS, China began the Tiangong program, operating two prototype stations before the current Tiangong, the world's third modular space station.  
Most space operations are managed by the China National Space Administration and the People's Liberation Army Aerospace Force, which directs the Astronaut Corps and the Chinese Deep Space Network. China's Moon missions achieved the world's first far side landing (Chang'e-4), longest-lived rover (Yutu-2 ), and sample returns (Chang'e-5 and -6). China's interplanetary missions deployed Tianwen-1, a Mars orbiter, lander, and the rover Zhurong; Tianwen-2 willl visit an asteroid and a comet from June 2026. Its satellite constellations include BeiDou Navigation and Gaofen Observation. Long March 5 is the most powerful rocket operating outside of the US, and first Long March to not use hypergolic propellant.
Since 2019, China's private and state enterprises have made over a hundred launches with new rocket designs, such as the Kuaizhou family and Ceres-1. China is developing reusable spacecraft, such as the Zhuque-3 rocket and a clandestine spaceplane. China plans a crewed lunar landing by 2030, using the under-development Mengzhou craft, Lanyue lander, and Long March 10 rocket. From 2031, China aims to construct International Lunar Research Station surface base, collaborating with Roscosmos and 11 other countries; China previously cooperated with the European Space Agency on deep space tracking and space science. The Tianwen-3 robotic Mars sample-return is scheduled to launch in 2028, the Tianwen-4 Jupiter orbiter and Uranus flyby in 2029, and the Shensuo probes aim to flyby Neptune.
